Django will actually support Python 2.7 throughout Django 1.11 LTS which should be supported until (at least) April 2020. So all nice Django package authors are stuck with supporting that, too, if we want to be human to people who haven't migrated to Python 3 yet. So, PyPy doesn't really cost anything to support, and the PyPy project might well get PyPy working with Python >= 3.5 well before 2020. They have also talked a lot about supporting Python 2 and Python 3 with the same code base (and the same JIT).

I, too, use PyPy with some projects and it just always sucks when package support is dropped when it doesn't cost anything to keep - if you support Python 2.7, supporting PyPy is always no-effort with existing test infrastructure.

For this method, and all of the methods which take `username` as a parameter, we could make it an optional keyword argument. Since the username is embedded in the access token itself, we can assume that as the default.

See https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/blob/master/mapbox/services/base.py#L59

In this case we'll need to shuffle the input arguments. Here's a rough sktech of my idea:
```
def update_auth(self, authorization_id, username=None, scopes=None, note=None):
    if username is None:
        username = self.username
```

Let's use this pattern to provide a default username to this and all the other Token methods.

Agreed, having multiple in-memory copies of the same persistent object can be problematic.

The current design tries to address this by making DataSet append-only, so you can't overwrite or modify data written by some other process. You could step on metadata, though, and you could also add multiple copies of the same result.

We could require just a single copy of the DataSet, but for applications like plotting that might cause performance problems.

Would could also tag one copy as the master copy, and only allow updates at the master. In many ways that's the simplest way to solve the problem.
